0: <title>Life cycle assessment of dairy production systems in Inner Mongolia: reiterate LCA modeling approaches </title>
Susie Ruqun Wu 1,2 . Xinchao Liu 1,3 Lulu Wang1 Xiaoping Xin Jiquan Chen4 Changliang Shao
Received: 13 January 2021 / Accepted: 1 July 2021 / Published online: 13 July 2021
C The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2021
1: Purpose The selection of different LCA modeling approaches can lead to inconsistent applications and results. Here, we proposed decision trees to identify the most appropriate modeling approach (attributional/decisional/consequential LCA (ALCA/DLCA/CLCA)) and applied them to examine both direct and indirect impacts of different dairy production systems in Inner Mongolia (IM), China-a pastoral area subject to policy debates about modernizing traditional pastoralism systems characterized by low productivity.

Methods Decision trees to select appropriate LCA modeling approaches were developed by reviewing various international standards and guidelines. Using Hulunbuir grassland as the study site, three streamlined LCAs were then conducted with a functional unit of 1 kg fat-and-protein-corrected milk (FPCM) following the proposed approach. ALCA was used to examine the direct environmental impacts of the current extensive pastoralism system (EPS). DLCA was used to explore the direct environmental impacts of a future intensive production system (IPS). CLCA was applied to assess the direct and indirect environmental impacts of transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system (TPS).

Results and discussion EPS showed moderate environmental impacts associated with high uncertainty due to its low pro- ductivity and data variability among surveyed farms. Following DLCA and using presumed data from a dairy company's ESG report, IPS received relatively low impact scores due to a change in its energy structure. After incorporating indirect impacts, the CLCA results for TPS showed distinctive patterns with new environmental hotspots identified. Although the results were subject to limitations on data accuracy, a key policy implication lay in the shifting of environmental burdens due to decision making under both IPS and TPS following a sensitivity analysis.

Conclusions The importance of selecting an appropriate LCA modeling approach under different study goals was reiterated, followed by three case studies in IM, China. If the current EPS were to be replaced, careful planning would be needed to avoid environmental burden shifting, with both direct and indirect impacts needing to be assessed thoroughly. Alternative emis- sion reduction plans for EPS lie in controlling direct emissions and improving its production efficiency and energy structure.
Keywords Attributional LCA . Decisional LCA . Consequential LCA . Dairy production

5: <header>1 Introduction </header>

Projected population growth, lifestyle, and dietary changes, especially in fast-growing economies, are driving an increas- ing demand for dairy and meat products. The primary production systems supporting dairy and meat demands, however, contribute to many environmental problems. The global warming impact of ruminant livestock farms is par- ticularly high (Guerci et al. 2013); it is estimated that milk and beef production cause 9% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Styles et al. 2018). A robust and scientific assessment is needed to understand and balance the con- sumption and impacts of dairy and meat production, since the dairy production system is an integral part of the larger social-ecological system (Chen et al. 2018; Chen et al. 2020; Qi et al. 2017).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle-based approaches have been widely used as reference methods for assessing sustainability of food production systems (Takacs and Borrion 2020). Extensive efforts have been put into stud- ying the potential environmental impacts of dairy products. Some studies have analyzed impacts from individual pro- duction systems, while others have compared environmental impacts of intensive and pastoral farming systems (Baldini et al. 2017). Results of these studies are not conclusive, and are sometimes contradictory (Schueler and Paulsen 2018). Internationally recognized guidelines and standards, such as ISO 14,040 (ISO 2006), the ILCD Handbook (European Commission 2010), LEAP guidelines (LEAP 2016), IDF guidelines (IDF 2015), and product category rules (PCR)- Raw Milk (version 2.11) and the PCR Basic Module CPC 21 (version 3.01) from the International EPD System (The International EPD 2013, 2019), suggest different avenues for choosing an LCA modeling approach and selecting func- tional unit(s) (FU) and allocation methods.

Since LCA methodology is evolving, it is challenging to produce an easy-to-understand yet scientifically robust study which consumers, producers, and policy makers can rely on in making sustainable decisions. Thomassen et al. (2008) argue that arbitrary selection of LCA methods regardless of their applicability can cause difficulties in identifying envi- ronmentally friendly ways to produce milk and meat prod- ucts. They find that attributional LCA (ALCA) is valuable for identifying opportunities for reducing emissions within the life cycle or supply chain (i.e., hotspot analysis), whereas consequential LCA (CLCA) is superior at accounting for consequences, especially indirect effects caused by a change in product demand. Notably, the choice of LCA modeling approach can lead to large variations and controversial con- clusions or decisions (Schueler and Paulsen 2018).

As one of the major pastoral areas in China, Inner Mon- golia (IM) is a leader of the dairy industry, accounting for one-third of total domestic milk production in 2019. This major production system, nevertheless, extensively relies on the small-scale pastoralism farming of approximately 3.2 million herdsmen households in the region (Inner Mongolia Bureau of Statistics 2018). The traditional pastoralism sys- tem in IM is characterized by low productivity, severe deg- radation of grasslands, extremely low income for herders, and poor living conditions. By the end of 2016, only 4% of villagers had access to natural gas, while the rest depended on burning coal as their major heating source during the long severe winters.

Various proposed policies point to an urgent need to upgrade the current production system, restore the grass- land ecosystem, and reconstruct a sustainable social and eco- nomic structure in IM. Proposed solutions include replacing the existing extensive pastoralism system with an industri- alized high-efficiency production system and restructuring industry by developing low-pollution industries such as eco-tourism. To understand environmental impacts of cur- rent milk production under pastoral farming and potential impacts of a transition to modern intensive dairy farming systems, the objective of this study was to (1) reiterate dif- ferent LCA modeling approaches and propose decision trees to aid the selection of the most appropriate approach and (2) perform three streamlined LCAs following the proposed modeling approaches to understand current and potential, direct, and indirect impacts when modernizing the pastoral- ism system in IM.

Proposed solutions include replacing the existing extensive pastoralism system with an industri- alized high-efficiency production system and restructuring industry by developing low-pollution industries such as eco-tourism. To understand environmental impacts of cur- rent milk production under pastoral farming and potential impacts of a transition to modern intensive dairy farming systems, the objective of this study was to (1) reiterate dif- ferent LCA modeling approaches and propose decision trees to aid the selection of the most appropriate approach and (2) perform three streamlined LCAs following the proposed modeling approaches to understand current and potential, direct, and indirect impacts when modernizing the pastoral- ism system in IM.
10: We first delimited two widely applied LCA modeling approaches (i.e., ALCA and CLCA) following the interna- tional guidelines and standards for dairy production. A third modeling approach, decisional LCA (DLCA), developed by

Frischknecht and Stucki (2010), was also reviewed to explore its potential use for dairy LCA. We then developed a deci- sion tree to facilitate the selection of a modeling approach. Solutions to the major technical challenge for LCA studies of dairy farming-allocation among co-products-were also provided. Our decision trees were constructed hierarchically, with stepwise instructions of various sub-modules when necessary. Such a modular approach enables practitioners to easily navigate and find the sub-module(s) for their own needs.
11: <header>2.1 Selection of an appropriate modeling approach </header>

ALCA usually begins with a clearly defined single-product system (LEAP 2016) and a system boundary that "defines the unit processes to be included in the system" (ISO 2006). The system is usually described in a process flowchart, e.g., linking unit processes (e.g., feed inputs, forage crop inputs, livestock inputs) for milk production (IDF 2015). Some unit processes can be left out of system boundaries in accordance with the cutoff criteria (ISO 2006).

CLCA theoretically assumes "the whole world as it is" and adopts the "no system boundary" concept because all activities induced by changes in the studied product sys- tem are included (De Camillis et al. 2013). Such indirect effects may occur via teleconnection during intensification transitions when certain types of farm systems replace oth- ers (Styles et al. 2018). It is argued that only CLCA reflects physical and monetary causalities, since ALCA isolates the product system from the rest of the world by adopting cut- off rules and allocation (Consequential-LCA 2015). Once all affected processes are identified, CLCA uses a process flowchart to represent the system (Thomassen et al. 2008).

Relisting the detailed goals and grouping them into sev- eral categories (Table 1), it became clear that ALCA is the choice to address know-how questions that involve a single product system. ALCA is also preferable for communica- tion purposes. CLCA is applied exclusively when indirect and rebound effects are considered. ALCA is preferable for micro-level decision support, while CLCA is preferable for meso- and macro-level decision support, according to the ILCD Handbook (European Commission 2010). For bench- marking and comparison studies, both LEAP and IDF exclu- sively exclude the use of CLCA. However, Weidema (2003) argues that CLCA shall be used for comparison purposes.

To resolve the contradictions, we referred to DLCA, in which different LCA modeling approaches are selected based on the "relative economic size" of the product system, by quantifying a decision support as micro, meso, macro, or strategic. DLCA is proposed as an "alternative definition of the CLCA on the micro-economy level." However, it does not encourage "avoided-burden" allocations, as CLCA does (Frischknecht and Stucki 2010; De Camillis et al. 2013).

In practice, DLCA follows the ALCA approach except for using different linking algorithms between interlinked pro- cesses to determine environmental impacts from suppliers (Frischknecht and Stucki 2010). DLCA uses the "actual or anticipated financial and contractual relations between eco- nomic actors (business-to-business (B2B) relations)" instead of the average technology mix used in ALCA (Fig. 1).

We adopted the methods of Frischknecht and Stucki (2010) to determine the scale of a system: the relative share of the contribution of the studied system to a reference "total," which can be defined economically (the total of a certain economic sector) or politically (the total of a country or a region). Thresholds of 0.1% and 1% of the relative eco- nomic size are applied to delimit small-, medium-, and large- scale effects arising from the studied systems (Frischknecht and Stucki 2010). For the dairy sector, various choices of the total may be defined, such as using the totals of economic sector or political entities (Frischknecht and Stucki 2010). We used the latter and proposed three options for determin- ing the scale of a product system depending on the business nature of the studied system itself (Fig. 2).

14: Standards and guidelines differ greatly in how they treat allocation, which echoes the issue of LCA studies not handling co-products consistently. The LEAP (2016) requires practitioners to exclude substitution as an allo- cation option. The IDF (2015) recommends biological allocation, highlighting the use of feed energy. Both the PCR-Raw Milk and PCR Basic Module CPC 21 follow the

allocation procedures of ISO 14,040 (2006). In practice, researchers have applied various allocation rules when studying milk production. Baldini et al. (2017) found that over half of reviewed studies chose economic alloca- tion. For ALCA, various guidelines (e.g., the two PCRs, LEAP (2016) and IDF (2015)) propose partition methods (Fig. SI.2). In CLCA, three co-production systems are cat- egorized (supplementary information Table SI.1) based on the technological connections between co-products (Majeau-Bettez et al. 2018), which serves as the start- ing point from which to choose an appropriate allocation method (Fig. 3). Details on the allocation methods are provided in Table SI.1, Figs. SI.1 and SI.2.
15: Three streamlined LCAs were conducted following the pro- posed modeling approach to understand direct environmen- tal impacts of the current extensive pastoralism system of cattle in IM as well as potential and indirect impacts under a scenario of transition toward intensive cattle farming (Table 2).

Case 3: to assess direct and indirect environmental impacts caused by the transition from EPS to IPS: a tran- sitional production system (TPS)

Study site Farms currently adopting EPS are located in Xeltala and Teni counties (120.021-120.1469 E, 49.349- 49.481° N, mean elevation 640-650 m) in the northeast of Hailar District, Hulunbuir City, IM, which has a cold semi-arid climate, a mean frost-free period of 90-100 days, and annual precipitation of 300 mm. Xeltala and Teni farms cover an area of 595 and 1900 km², respectively, with a total of 20,400 and 80,000 ha of cultivated land, and 36,300 and 44,700 ha of pastoral land. Livestock production there adopts grazing, mainly from June 1 to November 1, com- bined with domestic feeding using self-produced hay as well as purchased hay and feedstock as the main forage input dur- ing non-grazing seasons. The IPS was assumed to be estab- lished within the Hulunbuir grassland region, as explained in case 2.

21: <header>2.3.2 Life cycle inventory, data source, and assumption </header>

Case 1: the current extensive pastoralism system A stream- lined farm-gate LCA was conducted for EPS, using farm sur- vey data from eight EPS farms in the study area. The survey was conducted in 2018. The FU was 1 kg of fat-and-protein- corrected milk (FPCM) produced onsite. The overall produc- tivity was low, with annual milk production of 2400 kg raw milk (or 2282 kg FPCM) per milking cow. This was about half of the average production volume per cow in China (Textor 2021). Unlike intensive farming where high-yield Holstein cattle are raised, local farmers rely heavily on local breeds such as Sanhe and/or Mongolian cattle because of less requirements for feeding and housing. The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

Fig. 4 System boundaries for the three streamlined farm-gate LCA. Case 1 applied ALCA to study current EPS (system boundary indi- cated by the blue box). Case 2 applied DLCA to study IPS (system boundary indicated by the orange box). Case 3 applied CLCA to

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

2.3.2.1 Data gaps and assumptions Primary and secondary data were obtained from various sources (Table 3). Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). LCA data granularity and unit processes were categorized for each case following its unique scope (Table 3).

No first-tier transportation data were available, due to either the upstream suppliers' reluctance to provide infor- mation under EPS or a lack of data for the hypothetical IPS and TPS. Consequently, neither local nor domestic transpor- tation of materials was included for the three cases. For the IPS and TPS using imported feed from various countries, as well as the imported marginal beef, all trans-continental transportation was included, assuming sea freight container ships for the feedstock and container ships with cooling for the imported beef.

For all three case studies, water consumption was not recorded due to lack of data. A secondary data source (e.g., ecoinvent database) was not used, since water consumption associated with forage production varies widely among data sources (e.g., Hoekstra and Hung 2002; Wernet et al. 2016), and no accurate data source that fit the climate and soil con- ditions in the study area were available.

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).
22: <header>2.3.2 Life cycle inventory, data source, and assumption </header>

Case 1: the current extensive pastoralism system A stream- lined farm-gate LCA was conducted for EPS, using farm sur- vey data from eight EPS farms in the study area. The survey was conducted in 2018. The FU was 1 kg of fat-and-protein- corrected milk (FPCM) produced onsite. The overall produc- tivity was low, with annual milk production of 2400 kg raw milk (or 2282 kg FPCM) per milking cow. This was about half of the average production volume per cow in China (Textor 2021). Unlike intensive farming where high-yield Holstein cattle are raised, local farmers rely heavily on local breeds such as Sanhe and/or Mongolian cattle because of less requirements for feeding and housing. The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). No first-tier transportation data were available, due to either the upstream suppliers' reluctance to provide infor- mation under EPS or a lack of data for the hypothetical IPS and TPS. Consequently, neither local nor domestic transpor- tation of materials was included for the three cases. For the IPS and TPS using imported feed from various countries, as well as the imported marginal beef, all trans-continental transportation was included, assuming sea freight container ships for the feedstock and container ships with cooling for the imported beef.

For all three case studies, water consumption was not recorded due to lack of data. A secondary data source (e.g., ecoinvent database) was not used, since water consumption associated with forage production varies widely among data sources (e.g., Hoekstra and Hung 2002; Wernet et al. 2016), and no accurate data source that fit the climate and soil con- ditions in the study area were available.

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).
24: <header>2.3.2 Life cycle inventory, data source, and assumption </header>

Case 1: the current extensive pastoralism system A stream- lined farm-gate LCA was conducted for EPS, using farm sur- vey data from eight EPS farms in the study area. The survey was conducted in 2018. The FU was 1 kg of fat-and-protein- corrected milk (FPCM) produced onsite. The overall produc- tivity was low, with annual milk production of 2400 kg raw milk (or 2282 kg FPCM) per milking cow. This was about half of the average production volume per cow in China (Textor 2021). Unlike intensive farming where high-yield Holstein cattle are raised, local farmers rely heavily on local breeds such as Sanhe and/or Mongolian cattle because of less requirements for feeding and housing. The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

Fig. 4 System boundaries for the three streamlined farm-gate LCA. Case 1 applied ALCA to study current EPS (system boundary indi- cated by the blue box). Case 2 applied DLCA to study IPS (system boundary indicated by the orange box). Case 3 applied CLCA to

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

2.3.2.1 Data gaps and assumptions Primary and secondary data were obtained from various sources (Table 3). Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). LCA data granularity and unit processes were categorized for each case following its unique scope (Table 3).

Table 3 LCI data granularity and assumptions, farm and milk production capacity for EPS, IPS, and TPS. Detailed LCI data can be found in the SI Excel (LCI_EPSIPSTPS.xlsx) and the cal- culation files (with file extension ".ipynb")

The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

Fig. 4 System boundaries for the three streamlined farm-gate LCA. Case 1 applied ALCA to study current EPS (system boundary indi- cated by the blue box). Case 2 applied DLCA to study IPS (system boundary indicated by the orange box). Case 3 applied CLCA to

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

2.3.2.1 Data gaps and assumptions Primary and secondary data were obtained from various sources (Table 3). Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). LCA data granularity and unit processes were categorized for each case following its unique scope (Table 3).

No first-tier transportation data were available, due to either the upstream suppliers' reluctance to provide infor- mation under EPS or a lack of data for the hypothetical IPS and TPS. Consequently, neither local nor domestic transpor- tation of materials was included for the three cases. For the IPS and TPS using imported feed from various countries, as well as the imported marginal beef, all trans-continental transportation was included, assuming sea freight container ships for the feedstock and container ships with cooling for the imported beef.

For all three case studies, water consumption was not recorded due to lack of data. A secondary data source (e.g., ecoinvent database) was not used, since water consumption associated with forage production varies widely among data sources (e.g., Hoekstra and Hung 2002; Wernet et al. 2016), and no accurate data source that fit the climate and soil con- ditions in the study area were available.

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).

No first-tier transportation data were available, due to either the upstream suppliers' reluctance to provide infor- mation under EPS or a lack of data for the hypothetical IPS and TPS. Consequently, neither local nor domestic transpor- tation of materials was included for the three cases. For the IPS and TPS using imported feed from various countries, as well as the imported marginal beef, all trans-continental transportation was included, assuming sea freight container ships for the feedstock and container ships with cooling for the imported beef.

For all three case studies, water consumption was not recorded due to lack of data. A secondary data source (e.g., ecoinvent database) was not used, since water consumption associated with forage production varies widely among data sources (e.g., Hoekstra and Hung 2002; Wernet et al. 2016), and no accurate data source that fit the climate and soil con- ditions in the study area were available.

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).
29: <header>2.3.2 Life cycle inventory, data source, and assumption </header>

Case 1: the current extensive pastoralism system A stream- lined farm-gate LCA was conducted for EPS, using farm sur- vey data from eight EPS farms in the study area. The survey was conducted in 2018. The FU was 1 kg of fat-and-protein- corrected milk (FPCM) produced onsite. The overall produc- tivity was low, with annual milk production of 2400 kg raw milk (or 2282 kg FPCM) per milking cow. This was about half of the average production volume per cow in China (Textor 2021). Unlike intensive farming where high-yield Holstein cattle are raised, local farmers rely heavily on local breeds such as Sanhe and/or Mongolian cattle because of less requirements for feeding and housing. The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

Fig. 4 System boundaries for the three streamlined farm-gate LCA. Case 1 applied ALCA to study current EPS (system boundary indi- cated by the blue box). Case 2 applied DLCA to study IPS (system boundary indicated by the orange box). Case 3 applied CLCA to

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

2.3.2.1 Data gaps and assumptions Primary and secondary data were obtained from various sources (Table 3). Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). LCA data granularity and unit processes were categorized for each case following its unique scope (Table 3).

Table 3 LCI data granularity and assumptions, farm and milk production capacity for EPS, IPS, and TPS. Detailed LCI data can be found in the SI Excel (LCI_EPSIPSTPS.xlsx) and the cal- culation files (with file extension ".ipynb")

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).
31: <header>2.3.2 Life cycle inventory, data source, and assumption </header>

Case 1: the current extensive pastoralism system A stream- lined farm-gate LCA was conducted for EPS, using farm sur- vey data from eight EPS farms in the study area. The survey was conducted in 2018. The FU was 1 kg of fat-and-protein- corrected milk (FPCM) produced onsite. The overall produc- tivity was low, with annual milk production of 2400 kg raw milk (or 2282 kg FPCM) per milking cow. This was about half of the average production volume per cow in China (Textor 2021). Unlike intensive farming where high-yield Holstein cattle are raised, local farmers rely heavily on local breeds such as Sanhe and/or Mongolian cattle because of less requirements for feeding and housing. The functions of EPS were mainly twofold: producing cattle meat and milk.

The production ratio of meat to milk varied widely among farms, with milk representing a mean of ca. 60% of total annual income. Economic allocation was used for each farm. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consumption from onsite processes and associated emissions and resource consumption from upstream processes (Fig. 4).

Case 2: a future intensive production system China Modern Dairy Holdings Limited (MD) is one of the largest dairy farming operators and fresh raw milk producers in China in terms of herd size and milk yield (MD 2020a). MD co- operates 14 large dairy farms in northern and central China, each with 10,000-40,000 head. IPS assumed that MD would expand by one farm in Hulunbuir grassland, where the company has not yet established operations. A streamlined farm-gate LCA was conducted for IPS with a 10,000-head capacity. The FU was 1 kg of FPCM produced, using eco- nomic allocation. The IPS productivity was about twice as much as the average production volume per cow in China, with annual milk production of 10.1 t raw milk (or 10.02 t FPCM) per milking cow (MD 2019). Based on MD's milk production capacity, its new farm could provide approxi- mately 0.4% of annual domestic milk production in China. Following module 1 and sub-module 1 (Fig. 1), DLCA was suitable for assessing its environmental impacts. The system boundary included direct emissions and resource consump- tion from onsite processes and upstream processes (Fig. 4). Estimation of inventory data followed "actual or anticipated B2B relations," and activity data were collected from MD's 2018 and 2019 ESG report (MD 2019, 2020b), its 2019 annual report (MD 2020a), and other sources (Table 3, SI Excel "IPS_MD_ESG" sheet).
Case 3: transitioning EPS to IPS-a transitional production system Assuming that IPS was approved and supported by local authorities, it would undoubtedly influence EPS

Fig. 4 System boundaries for the three streamlined farm-gate LCA. Case 1 applied ALCA to study current EPS (system boundary indi- cated by the blue box). Case 2 applied DLCA to study IPS (system boundary indicated by the orange box). Case 3 applied CLCA to

study both direct and indirect impacts from TPS (system boundary indicated by the green box). Overseas transportation (T) of materials was included for IPS and TPS with all local and domestic transporta- tion excluded for all three cases

and the local social-ecological structure. To thoroughly understand the direct and indirect impacts of TPS and to support policy making, a CLCA was conducted with the system boundary expanded (Fig. 4). First, the number of herder households that would be displaced through IPS was estimated using the mean herd size per household surveyed in EPS, which suggested that 500 EPS farms would be displaced. Applying an annual milk production of 29.67 FPCM and 2.17 t liveweight meat production from an aver- age EPS farm, the displaced milk and meat production per 1 kg FPCM under TPS was 0.25 kg FPCM and 0.02 kg meat (SI Excel "TPS_displaced" sheet).

While IPS could produce more milk than EPS, the resulting displaced meat production needed to be sup- plied by marginal suppliers, assuming there would be an increasing demand for beef in China in the coming dec- ades. Following the allocation logic in module 2, prod- uct substitution allocation (PSA) with a reversed sign was applied, since the marginal beef production would be an added impact (instead of avoided burden) of TPS.
Meat imported from Brazil (fed mainly on pasture) was assumed as the marginal supplier for the displaced beef from EPS (SI calculation file: "CLCA_default_SA2. ipynb").

2.3.2.1 Data gaps and assumptions Primary and secondary data were obtained from various sources (Table 3). Other than direct consumption and emission data specified for unit processes, all upstream data for manufacturing of input materials and energy, transpor- tation, resource input, and emissions associated with background processes were sourced from ecoinvent databases. Whenever a geographically specific dataset was available for a specific unit process (e.g., electricity consumption, feed production), it was adopted with ref- erence to the local data. Otherwise, proxy data from the world average were used (SI calculation files, with file extension ".ipynb"). LCA data granularity and unit processes were categorized for each case following its unique scope (Table 3).

No first-tier transportation data were available, due to either the upstream suppliers' reluctance to provide infor- mation under EPS or a lack of data for the hypothetical IPS and TPS. Consequently, neither local nor domestic transpor- tation of materials was included for the three cases. For the IPS and TPS using imported feed from various countries, as well as the imported marginal beef, all trans-continental transportation was included, assuming sea freight container ships for the feedstock and container ships with cooling for the imported beef.

For all three case studies, water consumption was not recorded due to lack of data. A secondary data source (e.g., ecoinvent database) was not used, since water consumption associated with forage production varies widely among data sources (e.g., Hoekstra and Hung 2002; Wernet et al. 2016), and no accurate data source that fit the climate and soil con- ditions in the study area were available.

While the best-available data were used for the stream- lined LCAs, many were subject to precision and accuracy issues. Important inventory flows mentioned above were not included due to lack of data. Surveys of local farmers were based mainly on their experiences (i.e., past knowl- edge). Unit production data for IPS were downscaled esti- mates from company-level data. For purchased electricity, we used a conservative estimate of its renewable percentage for both IPS and TPS, since the maximum capacity of wind energy was 92% of the local electricity supply according to the monitoring data shown by the State Grid East Inner Mongolia Electric Power Company, Ltd. (SASAC 2013). For the feed supply, actual/anticipated B2B relations were assumed for IPS using expert input. Due to a lack of a Chi- nese industry-specific database, the marginal suppliers and supply mix under TPS were identified from FAO data using its forecast up to 2028 (OECD-FAO 2019a, 2019b).
32: The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) phase models certain environmental issues, called impact categories, and

uses category indicators to explain LCI results. This study assessed nine impact categories from ReCiPe 2016, fol- lowing its midpoint Hierarchist approach (Huijbregts et al. 2016). The impact assessment was performed using Bright- way2 (Mutel 2017) after importing the ReCiPe 2016 (https:// pypi.org/project/bw-recipe-2016) to the brightway2 python package. Some impact categories were omitted for the sake of conciseness, and those presented here are considered to be most closely related to the agricultural system studied (Table 4).
33: Sensitivity analysis was conducted for IPS with differ- ent scenarios of feed input and for TPS with different marginal suppliers for the displaced beef. For IPS, fol- lowing MD's forage policy of "zero distance between forage grass planting and cow breeding and milk process- ing" (MD 2019), the default scenario assumed that 60% of the total feed is produced onsite, utilizing fertilizers from its own biogas plant. Nevertheless, IPS may need to rely on more purchased feed rather than self-produced forage because current policies in IM do not allow natu- ral grasslands to be transformed. We thus adjusted the feed structure for IPS and reduced the assumed onsite forage production from 60 to 30% since existing man- made pastures can still be transformed for onsite forage production.

For TPS, meat production from the current EPS was con- sidered as another marginal supplier for the displaced beef, because it may have two advantages compared to importing beef from Brazil: (1) no international freight shipping (with cooling) was needed and (2) the displaced herder households who had engaged in the EPS could continue to produce beef with the surplus land and labor.
Uncertainty analysis was conducted for all three cases using Monte-Carlo simulation with 5000 runs. Uncertainty information for the foreground data of EPS, IPS, and TPS

1: EPS had moderate environmental impacts due to its low pro- ductivity. Energy and feedstock consumption were the two main environmental hotspots for EP, ETP, LTP, ODP, and SOP (Fig. 5a). For energy consumption, coal dominated, while diesel consumption was low. Purchased electricity also contributed little. Purchased feedstock dominated almost all impact for LTP and ODP and contributed the most to ETP (ca. 61%) and SOP (ca. 71%). Contributions from onsite processes varied greatly among impact categories. Onsite animal emissions contributed the most to AP (ca. 64%), fol- lowed by GWP and PMFP due to the direct NH3 emissions during grazing and CH4 emissions from enteric fermenta- tion of animals. See SI section III for a detailed contribution analysis for each process.
The uncertainty analysis revealed high variability among impacts (Fig. 6) due to the large variation among EPS farm

practices (SI Excel "EPS_survey" and "EPS_LCI" sheets). Because a triangular distribution was used to model fore- ground data uncertainty from surveyed farms, and most background data from the ecoinvent database had a log- normal distribution, the resulting median impacts were higher than the deterministic LCA results, since "uncer- tainty increases the central tendency of the indicator result" (Muller et al. 2017).
2: <header>3.1 EPS </header>

EPS had moderate environmental impacts due to its low pro- ductivity. Energy and feedstock consumption were the two main environmental hotspots for EP, ETP, LTP, ODP, and SOP (Fig. 5a). For energy consumption, coal dominated, while diesel consumption was low. Purchased electricity also contributed little. Purchased feedstock dominated almost all impact for LTP and ODP and contributed the most to ETP (ca. 61%) and SOP (ca. 71%). Contributions from onsite processes varied greatly among impact categories. Onsite animal emissions contributed the most to AP (ca. 64%), fol- lowed by GWP and PMFP due to the direct NH3 emissions during grazing and CH4 emissions from enteric fermenta- tion of animals. See SI section III for a detailed contribution analysis for each process.
The uncertainty analysis revealed high variability among impacts (Fig. 6) due to the large variation among EPS farm

practices (SI Excel "EPS_survey" and "EPS_LCI" sheets). Because a triangular distribution was used to model fore- ground data uncertainty from surveyed farms, and most background data from the ecoinvent database had a log- normal distribution, the resulting median impacts were higher than the deterministic LCA results, since "uncer- tainty increases the central tendency of the indicator result" (Muller et al. 2017).
3: <header>3.2 IPS </header>

Overall, IPS had relatively low environmental impacts per 1 kg of FPCM due to its production efficiency (Fig. 7). Purchased feedstock led all impact categories except GWP, LOP, and LTP, and contributed most to EP, ETP, and SOP (Fig. 5b). Contribution from energy consump- tion was relatively low, with slightly higher impacts from onsite biogas production to ODP (ca. 25%), and purchased electricity to GWP and ETP. Onsite emissions made high- est contribution to GWP due to enteric fermentation (ca. 35%). Trans-continental transportation of purchased feeds also contributed greatly to selected impact categories, accounting for ca. 33% and 27% of total PMFP and AP, respectively.

In the sensitivity analysis, reducing onsite forage in the feed mix while relying more on purchased feed resulted in higher impacts for all categories except LTP, due to higher impacts from production and transportation of the purchased feed. This result seems to confirm MD's policy decision to rely to a certain extent on onsite forage production.

LCIA abbreviation AP: Terrestrial acidification potential; EP: Freshwater eutrophication potential; ETP: Terrestrial ecotoxicity potential; GWP: Global warming potential; LOP: Land occupation potential; LTP: Land transformation potential; OPD: Ozone depletion potential; PMFP Particulate matter formation potential; SOP: Surplus ore potential

Fig. 5 LCIA results per 1 kg FPCM and process contributions under EPS (left a), IPS (middle b), and TPS (right c). a EPS with the default scenario using data collected from farm surveys. b IPS with the default scenario assuming 60% of feed consumption from onsite forage production while the "newfeed" scenario reducing

onsite forage production from 60 to 30% c TPS with the default sce- nario assuming marginal beef supplier as that imported from Brazil ("BRbeef") while the "EPSbeef" scenario assuming surplus land and labor would be used for beef production under current EPS conditions
4: <header>3.2 IPS </header>

Overall, IPS had relatively low environmental impacts per 1 kg of FPCM due to its production efficiency (Fig. 7). Purchased feedstock led all impact categories except GWP, LOP, and LTP, and contributed most to EP, ETP, and SOP (Fig. 5b). Contribution from energy consump- tion was relatively low, with slightly higher impacts from onsite biogas production to ODP (ca. 25%), and purchased electricity to GWP and ETP. Onsite emissions made high- est contribution to GWP due to enteric fermentation (ca. 35%). Trans-continental transportation of purchased feeds also contributed greatly to selected impact categories, accounting for ca. 33% and 27% of total PMFP and AP, respectively.

In the sensitivity analysis, reducing onsite forage in the feed mix while relying more on purchased feed resulted in higher impacts for all categories except LTP, due to higher impacts from production and transportation of the purchased feed. This result seems to confirm MD's policy decision to rely to a certain extent on onsite forage production.

Fig. 5 LCIA results per 1 kg FPCM and process contributions under EPS (left a), IPS (middle b), and TPS (right c). a EPS with the default scenario using data collected from farm surveys. b IPS with the default scenario assuming 60% of feed consumption from onsite forage production while the "newfeed" scenario reducing

onsite forage production from 60 to 30% c TPS with the default sce- nario assuming marginal beef supplier as that imported from Brazil ("BRbeef") while the "EPSbeef" scenario assuming surplus land and labor would be used for beef production under current EPS conditions
7: <header>3.2 IPS </header>

Overall, IPS had relatively low environmental impacts per 1 kg of FPCM due to its production efficiency (Fig. 7). Purchased feedstock led all impact categories except GWP, LOP, and LTP, and contributed most to EP, ETP, and SOP (Fig. 5b). Contribution from energy consump- tion was relatively low, with slightly higher impacts from onsite biogas production to ODP (ca. 25%), and purchased electricity to GWP and ETP. Onsite emissions made high- est contribution to GWP due to enteric fermentation (ca. 35%). Trans-continental transportation of purchased feeds also contributed greatly to selected impact categories, accounting for ca. 33% and 27% of total PMFP and AP, respectively.

In the sensitivity analysis, reducing onsite forage in the feed mix while relying more on purchased feed resulted in higher impacts for all categories except LTP, due to higher impacts from production and transportation of the purchased feed. This result seems to confirm MD's policy decision to rely to a certain extent on onsite forage production.

In the sensitivity analysis, reducing onsite forage in the feed mix while relying more on purchased feed resulted in higher impacts for all categories except LTP, due to higher impacts from production and transportation of the purchased feed. This result seems to confirm MD's policy decision to rely to a certain extent on onsite forage production.

10: Overall, IPS had relatively low environmental impacts per 1 kg of FPCM due to its production efficiency (Fig. 7). Purchased feedstock led all impact categories except GWP, LOP, and LTP, and contributed most to EP, ETP, and SOP (Fig. 5b). Contribution from energy consump- tion was relatively low, with slightly higher impacts from onsite biogas production to ODP (ca. 25%), and purchased electricity to GWP and ETP. Onsite emissions made high- est contribution to GWP due to enteric fermentation (ca. 35%). Trans-continental transportation of purchased feeds also contributed greatly to selected impact categories, accounting for ca. 33% and 27% of total PMFP and AP, respectively.

In the sensitivity analysis, reducing onsite forage in the feed mix while relying more on purchased feed resulted in higher impacts for all categories except LTP, due to higher impacts from production and transportation of the purchased feed. This result seems to confirm MD's policy decision to rely to a certain extent on onsite forage production.

(PSA was applied instead (Fig. 3)). The avoided impacts from the displaced milk production exceeded the added impacts from the marginal beef production due to the high environmental impacts of EPS milk for key impact categories, including AP, EP, and LOP (Fig. 5c). Impacts from energy showed distinctive patterns, especially impacts from biogas, which made negative contributions to PMFP and EP, due to the choice of marginal suppli- ers and technology mixes in the ecoinvent consequential database.

When the marginal beef supplier was switched in the sensitivity analysis from Brazilian imports to the EPS, EPS beef production had lower environmental impacts than Brazilian beef production, except for AP, EP, and SOP (Fig. 5c). The uncertainty analysis revealed much higher variability in all impact categories compared to that in the DLCA modeling approach for IPS, even though

the same uncertainty in foreground data (i.e., electric- ity consumption) was defined (SI Excel "IPS LCI" and "EPS_LCI" sheets). For example, 95% of the GWP scores were 1.02-3.02 kg CO2 eq. under TPS (Fig. 8), compared to 1.11-1.3 kg CO2 eq. under IPS (Fig. 7). This could be due to higher data variability in the background conse- quential database than in the cutoff database used for IPS.

2: <header>4 Discussion </header>

The final impacts of a farm-gate LCA per 1 kg FPCM pro- duced under different production systems varied greatly. We did not compare the results obtained from the three cases because each of them adopted different LCA mod- eling approach with its unique goal and scope. Instead, the case studies demonstrated proper selection of an appro- priate modeling approach under different scenarios and study goals.

The low productivity of traditional EPS explained the resulting moderate environmental impacts of the EPS milk. This finding aligns with heated debates over modernization of the current pastoralism system due to its low efficiency. In addition to inadequate farm management and direct open-air emissions, EPS showed high input of feedstock and energy per unit production. Traditional land use patterns have degraded grassland ecosystems, and this situation is further complicated by increasing demands for meat and milk from domestic and overseas markets, as well as competing land

use from the ever-growing surface mining in the region (Xu and Wu 2016). While it is suggested that such environmental degradation can be overcome through institutional changes and stakeholder engagement (Xu and Wu 2016), we believe that successful transition to sustainable production system for EPS remains a complex question for policy makers.

Sensitivity analysis of IPS and TPS revealed noteworthy patterns for policy making, especially if current EPS were to be replaced by IPS. While IPS appeared to produce envi- ronmental benefits across multiple impact categories, shifts to more onsite forage production can severely affect LTP in local ecosystems (Fig. 5b). Moreover, with an average annual precipitation of 380 mm in the region, it is highly impossible that natural water resources alone, without engaging extensive irrigation, could sustain onsite forage production in IPS. Selecting a different marginal beef sup- plier under TPS also shifted environmental burden among impact categories. Unlike EPS milk production, which has low productivity, EPS beef production has a relatively higher production ratio of meat to milk than IPS. Thus, by switch- ing the marginal supplier from Brazilian beef to EPS beef, significant environmental benefits were observed for GWP, ETP, LTP, ODP, and PMFP (Fig. 5c). Nevertheless, AP and EP increased due to extensive open-air emissions dur- ing grazing under EPS. Environmental tradeoffs need to be considered carefully during policy making.

The relatively low environmental impacts of IPS could be attributed largely to its improved energy structure, in which onsite biogas production could meet > 70% of the total energy demand of the company (MD 2019). This energy structure estimated for IPS adopted the "actual/contractual relationship" following the DLCA modeling approach. MD uses the concept of the "forage planting-cow breeding- fertilizer treatment-biogas power generation-fertilizer back to the field" industrial chain, and each of its farms is equipped with a large-scale biogas power generation facility in addition to treating onsite manure as organic fertilizer for nearby forage production sites and/or supplied to local farms (MD 2019). In contrast, another leading dairy farming company in China (also a listed company at the Hong Kong stock exchange)-YuanShengTa Dairy Farm Limited-has a very different energy structure according to its ESG report: coal consumption accounted for 64% of its total energy demand in 2019 (YuanShengTai 2020). While this huge within-industry variation was surprising, we did not adjust MD's energy data further following the DLCA modeling approach.

When considering indirect impacts under TPS, three new environmental hotspots emerged in addition to the purchased feed consumption: (1) additional beef produc- tion that was needed to replace the displaced EPS beef production, (2) avoided impacts from EPS milk production, and (3) biogas production. Both the indirect impacts of the

displaced milk/beef production, as well as the changing marginal supplier and different supply mix for feedstock and energy, contributed to the new pattern. Another indi- rect change not assessed here was the surplus labor from the displaced EPS and their subsequent economic activi- ties. Assuming that the new IPS would maintain MD's cur- rent production/employee ratio, hundreds of EPS farmers would lose the means to support themselves. A possible solution would be to engage this surplus labor in emerging industries within the region (e.g., eco-tourism). One can apply classic system expansion (Fig. 3) by including the added services/products produced by the surplus labor, or using alternate-activity allocation (Figs. 3 and SI.1) if the function is limited to milk production, as long as the two systems are completely independent (e.g., when engaging surplus labor in tourism).

The uncertainty analysis showed distinctive patterns among the three cases, in which data varied greatly for EPS and TPS but the least for IPS. This can be explained by uncertainty in the foreground production system and the background database. Data provided by each EPS farm sur- veyed indicated widely differing farming practices, from the resource-use rate and production efficiency to the production ratio of meat to milk. Some farms relied solely on forage produced onsite, while others used purchased feed exten- sively. The economic allocation factor among farms also varied greatly. All variability was recorded in foreground data during the ALCA modeling, which resulted in notably high uncertainty in the final ALCA results for EPS. For IPS and TPS, only electricity data had recorded uncertainty in the foreground data (SI Excel "IPS_LCI" and "TPS_LCI" sheets), with all other uncertainty coming from the back- ground database. We specifically modeled uncertainty for (1) biogas production, (2) purchased feed (e.g., alfalfa silage) production, and (3) electricity production (i.e., coal and wind energy sources) from the cutoff VS. consequential databases that were used for IPS and TPS, respectively. The results demonstrated that the high uncertainty observed for TPS was largely due to the higher data variability in the background consequential database, especially for biogas production.

One of the key limitations of the case studies was the accuracy of the raw data; nevertheless, we believe that the key conclusions stand. While IPS is equipped with emission- mitigation and clean-energy technology and has higher productivity, careful planning would be needed to avoid environmental burden shifting if EPS were to be replaced. Alternatively, more efficient emission-reduction plans for EPS exist, including (1) decreasing onsite animal emissions; (2) increasing production efficiency, especially material and energy consumption; and (3) restructuring energy sources, such as replacing open-air coal burning with other heating methods.

Joint Research Cen- tre Institute for Environment and Sustainability. https://doi.org/10. 2788/95227 "To explore direct environmental impacts under the current EPS, a potential new IPS, and the indirect impacts under TPS of 1 kg farm-gate FPCM, three streamlined LCAs were applied following the distinctive modeling approach rendered in the decision trees. Following ALCA, moderate environmental impacts were observed from EPS with high uncertainty, mainly due to its low productivity under current livestock management and land-use patterns. Following the DLCA approach of using ""actual or anticipated B2B rela- tions,"" IPS showed relatively low environmental impacts using presumed data from a dairy company's ESG report, due to its clean energy structure that uses biogas produced onsite. Adopting a CLCA approach, TPS revealed distinctive patterns with new environmental hotspots identified, such as added and avoided impacts from marginal beef production and displaced EPS milk production. Environmental burdens under IPS and TPS shifted when different decisions were made in sensitivity analysis. Careful planning is needed to avoid shifting of environmental burdens if EPS were to be replaced, with both direct and indirect impacts being assessed thoroughly. MD uses the concept of the "forage planting-cow breeding- fertilizer treatment-biogas power generation-fertilizer back to the field" industrial chain, and each of its farms is equipped with a large-scale biogas power generation facility in addition to treating onsite manure as organic fertilizer for nearby forage production sites and/or supplied to local farms (MD 2019).
